coredata.dc:title: Biogenic synthesis of hollow carbon dots using cigarette ash for photocatalytic and sensing applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Arpita,
Kumar, Parmod
Kumar, Ravi
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Khoo, Kuan Shiong
Kumar, Sandeep
coredata.dc:description: In this research article, the synthesis of hollow carbon dots (HCDs) from cigarette ash (CA) as a precursor using simple pyrolysis method is presented. The unique hollow nature of synthesized HCDs was confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and nitrogen physisorption techniques. HCDs, with a size of ∼ 9 nm, exhibit specific surface area of 12.1 m2/g and stimulated pore diameter of 2.1 nm. HCDs demonstrated remarkable degradation efficiency by eradicating 91 % of rose Bengal dye within 120 min under UV irradiation at pH = 5.00 and display selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a detection limit of around 1.1 µM. Ecological effect of HCDs on sprouted seeds of chickpea (Cicer arietinum) and wheat (Triticum aestivum) revealed their capacity to positively influence multiple aspects of plant development. Introduction of HCDs into growth medium (water) appeared to stimulate shoot, root, and leaf growth, suggesting a potential role for HCDs in enhancing overall plant health and productivity. These findings enabled HCDs derived from CA as promising candidate for sensitive metal ions detection, environmental remediation, and plant growth enhancement. It is the first time to upcycle CA into distinctive HCDs, serving as a potent tool for detecting and breaking down environmental contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hollow carbon dots
Photocatalytic activity
Nano probes
Biomass waste
Seed germination
Ecological study

Authors are also grateful to Department of Science & Technology, Delhi for providing financial assistance under DST PURSE Grant- SR/PURSE/2022/126.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for synthesis of HCDs.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum; (b) Tauc's plot and (c) emission spectrum of HCDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum and XPS analysis of HCDs: (b) survey (c) C 1 s (d) N1s (e) S 2p and (f) O 1 s spectra.
HRTEM images of HCDs at (a) low (b) medium (c) high magnification, (d) particle size distribution, (e) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, and (f) pore distribution size of HCDs.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectra of HCDs.

UV absorption spectra of encapsulation of dopamine hydrochloride by HCDs (Decrease in arrow indicates that concentration of DP has decreased in the solution which indicates the encapsulation of DP within HCDs).

(a) Selectivity of the HCD probe towards various metal ions, (b) Titrimetric studies of Fe3+ ions, (c) FRET mechanism of HCD sensing, and (d) Stern Volmer plot of relative fluorescence intensity versus ionic concentration.

Variation of percentage degradation of RB with time as a function of (a) HCDs concentration and (b) solution pH, (c) UV–vis spectrum of HCDs’ assisted RB degradation with time, and (e) Plot of kinetics for degradation.
(a) Comparative impact of radical scavengers on% degradation, (b) Reusability of HCDs for degradation of RB, and (e) Mechanism of degradation of RB in presence of HCDs.
Variation of average root length (a, d), average shoot length (b, e), and germination index (c, f) of chickpeas and wheat with increase in concentration of HCDs.
(a) HCDs’ assisted seed germination in wheat and chickpeas (b) Mechanism of enhanced seed germination in the presence of HCDs.
Parameters associated with cost of RB dye degradation using HCDs.
Details of HCDs synthesized and their applications.

head.ce:title: Biogenic synthesis of hollow carbon dots using cigarette ash for photocatalytic and sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Herbal Pharmacology and Environmental Sustainability, Chettinad Hospital and Research Institute, Chettinad Academy of Research and Education, Kelambakkam 603103, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dCentre for Herbal Pharmacology and Environmental Sustainability, Chettinad Hospital and Research Institute, Chettinad Academy of Research and Education, Kelambakkam 603103, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research article, the synthesis of hollow carbon dots (HCDs) from cigarette ash (CA) as a precursor using simple pyrolysis method is presented. The unique hollow nature of synthesized HCDs was confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and nitrogen physisorption techniques. HCDs, with a size of ∼ 9 nm, exhibit specific surface area of 12.1 m2/g and stimulated pore diameter of 2.1 nm. HCDs demonstrated remarkable degradation efficiency by eradicating 91 % of rose Bengal dye within 120 min under UV irradiation at pH = 5.00 and display selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a detection limit of around 1.1 µM. Ecological effect of HCDs on sprouted seeds of chickpea (Cicer arietinum) and wheat (Triticum aestivum) revealed their capacity to positively influence multiple aspects of plant development. Introduction of HCDs into growth medium (water) appeared to stimulate shoot, root, and leaf growth, suggesting a potential role for HCDs in enhancing overall plant health and productivity. These findings enabled HCDs derived from CA as promising candidate for sensitive metal ions detection, environmental remediation, and plant growth enhancement. It is the first time to upcycle CA into distinctive HCDs, serving as a potent tool for detecting and breaking down environmental contaminants.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a fascinating category of nanomaterials (NMs) in recent times with unique properties and promising applications in various fields. CDs, often referred to as carbon nanoparticles or quantum dots, are zero-dimensional carbon-based materials typically ranging in size from a few to several tens of nanometers. They exhibit exceptional optical properties, including strong fluorescence, high photostability, and tunable emission wavelengths, making them attractive for several types of applications including bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis [1–3]. CDs have the capability of generating reactive oxygen species under illumination with UV and visible light and thus can be used as an oxidant-antioxidant nanomaterial and photocatalyst [4]. Synthesis of CDs has been explored using different carbon sources, including biomass, organic compounds, and waste materials [2,5–7]. Biomass waste is widely used as a precursor for the synthesis of eco-friendly, and biocompatible CDs [3]. However, most biomass waste is currently unused, landfilled, or openly burned, which not only bring about a waste of resources but also cause some environmental problems threatening to human. In addition, cigarette smoking is a widespread habit that has led to significant environmental and health concerns e.g. neurological disorders (Parkinson's diseases, Alzheimer's diseases), cardiovascular diseases (hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus), pulmonary diseases (lung cancer, asthma) [8]. Cigarette smoking results in the generation of cigarette ash and cigarette butts which contribute to pollution and poses challenges for proper disposal. Bandi et al. (2018) used cigarette butts to synthesize N, S doped CDs and used for sensing Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid [9]. Using cigarette tobacco, Sinha et al. (2021) synthesized N-doped CDs and used for sensing picric acid. A nanocomposite was made from these CDs and multiwalled carbon nanotubes for UV-photodetection [10].

Water becomes unsafe for consumption and household use when numerous contaminants, notably some heavy hazardous metal ions and dyes, are present at elevated concentrations [11]. Because the existence of heavy metal ions and dyes in concentrations over permitted limits harms human health and raises the likelihood of developing cancer in central nervous system, liver, kidneys and lungs [12,13]. Therefore, it is critical to identify metal ions and the degradation of harmful dyes in water. Ferric ion (Fe3+) is a crucial part of many biological operations, including transportation of electrons and O2. Nevertheless, its presence in body in excess has a number of negative impacts. It can lead to Parkinson's disease, liver injury, Alzheimer's, heart disease, and many other diseases [1]. Therefore, accurate and sensitive probing of Fe3+ ions is of immense significance. Residual dyes and their associated by-products have been identified in the waste from textile plants. These substances are known to be carcinogenic and pose a threat to microorganisms, human health, and marine life. Currently, 100,000 distinct dyes are manufactured per year and textile industries globally utilize approximately 3.6 × 107 kg of dye each year. A significant concern arises during the dyeing process, where up to 20 % of the total global dye production is wasted and released into textile effluents [14]. RB, a commonly used synthetic dye, is known for its vibrant red color and extensive applications in various industries. However, its persistence, toxicity, and potential ecological risks have raised concerns about its safe disposal [15]. Therefore, efficient methods for the degradation of RB are highly desirable. Green fluorescent N-doped CDs synthesized by Rajapandi et al. (2023) using Ziziphus mauritiana (shrub) acted as photocatalyst for removal of Safranin-O dye with 79 % efficiency [16]. Annamalai et al. (2023) synthesized CDs from discarded dry cell batteries and effectively employed them as a photocatalyst under direct sunlight in presence of H2O2 for degradation of Bromothymol Blue (BTB). Within approximately 180 min, 84 % degradation of BTB was achieved [17]. Furthermore, despite applications of CDs in fields of metal detection and degradation of dyes, they are also promising candidates for seed germination [18]. The efficiency of chloroplast for light conversion is enhanced in the presence of light harvesting nanomaterials which further improves the photosynthesis rate [19]. Wang and colleagues conducted a chemical synthesis of pristine CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs. These NCDs exhibited pronounced efficacy as facilitators of light conversion and electron transfer, subsequently bolstering rate of photosynthesis resulting in increased crop yield [20]. Kou et al. synthesized CDs from glucose and l-cysteine which had exceptional antioxidant properties. The application outcomes validate these CDs' capacity to expedite seed germination, enhance drought resistance and augment water absorption in seeds. Furthermore, CDs elevate aquaporins gene expression, endowing tomato plants with robust resilience upon rehydration post-drought stress [21].

In this research work, we have explored the potential of the synthesized HCDs as a photocatalyst for degradation of RB, as a selective and sensitive nanoprobe for the detection of Fe3+ ions, and their role in plant growth. By repurposing CA, we aimed not only to provide a potential solution for waste management but also to develop a valuable material with unique properties. There are some research papers in which hollow CDs are synthesized but from chemical precursors discussed in Table 3. Herein, we have used CA which is a waste as a precursor for the synthesis of photoluminescent HCDs. Hollow structure of CDs provides a larger surface area compared to solid spheres with the same outer dimensions. This increased surface area offers more active sites for interactions with other molecules, leading to improved adsorption capacity, catalytic activity, and sensing performance. Furthermore, impact of HCDs on germination activity of sprouted chickpeas and wheat is investigated. The unique features of HCDs have significantly influenced anti-proliferative action of chickpeas and wheat seeds, particularly Germination Index values. This analysis provides valuable insights into their potential as plant growth enhancers. The findings of this research contribute to the development of innovative approaches for waste utilization, environmental remediation, and sustainable practices.

Chickpeas and wheat were purchased from the local market of Faridabad, India. Cigarette ash of local cigarette Flake (Liberty) has been used. Chemicals employed for stock solutions of metal ions such as CaCl2, MgCl2, CrCl3, BaCl2, KCl, ZnCl2, AgCl, FeCl2, CoCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, HgCl2 and FeCl3 purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Cigarette ash of local cigarette Flake (Liberty) has been used. All chemicals used were of analytical grade.

FTIR spectrum was obtained using Perkin Elmer FTIR (L1600300 Spectrum TWO LiTa, UK) with resolution of 0.5cm−1 in spectral region of 4500–550 cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectrum of HCDs was taken using Shimadzu UV-3600i PLUS spectrophotometer (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were performed using a Rigaku Ultima IV XRD (Japan) with scanning speed of 8 min−1 with Cu-Kα radiation (1.5418 A). HR-TEM (model- JEM 2100 plus, make- JEOL, Japan) was used to determine particle size at high resolution. Raman spectrum was obtained using Alpha300/ WITec (Germany) at 532 nm excitation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrumented by Thermo-Scientific model ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer (USA) operating with an Al-Kα monochromator powered at 1490 eV under an ultrahigh vacuum chamber (3.5 × 10−10 kPa). The sensing was performed at 340 nm excitation using Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, Japan. The BELSORP, N2 adsorption analyzer was used to calculate the BET surface area of the sample (Model-BELSORP-max, MicrotracBEL, Japan). The measurement was done after 15 h of degassing at 423 K. The pore-size distribution was analyzed by the adsorption branch of the isotherm, and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) technique was utilized to do so.

The preparation of CDs from CA involved a thermal calcination process. To begin synthesis, CA was placed in a silica crucible and subjected to a temperature of 350 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace (Fig. 1 ). After cooling, the grey-colored powder was collected from furnace and crushed to obtain a fine powder. Subsequently, 1 g of this fine powder was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water and stirred for 24 h followed by centrifugation for 12 min at 4000 rpm to get rid of aggregated particles. The supernatant was then filtered and evaporated to obtain HCDs powder.

In ecological assessment, sprouted seeds of both chickpeas and wheat were subjected to an experimental setup to evaluate influence of HCDs on their growth. This investigation aimed to understand how the presence of HCDs might affect various aspects of plant development. 0.2 g of HCDs were mixed with 200 mL of distilled water to obtain stock solution of 2000 ppm. Then this stock solution was diluted to get solution of 100, 250, 500 and 750 ppm solution. After thoroughly washing of chickpeas and wheat seeds with deionized water, these were soaked in distilled water for 24 h to facilitate sprouting. The sprouted seeds were subsequently placed into separate petri dishes, each containing varying concentrations of HCDs solution, with precisely 20 seeds in 35 mL of HCDs' solution per dish. For comparative analysis, another set of sprouted seeds was placed in petri dishes soaked in deionized water, serving as the control group. The assessment of root and shoot growth was conducted meticulously, involving the measurement of distinct pairs of seminal roots and shoots that emerged from the germinated seeds at both day 3 and day 6 of the experiment. To discern the primary impact of the synthesized HCDs, a range of parameters including average root length, shoot length, germination percentage, germination index, percentage inhibition of growth, and Vigor index I were calculated using designated formulas throughout the seed germination process [22,23]. Eq. (1) shows the formula for germination ability.
(1)
Germination
Ability
( % )
=
Number
of
germinated
seeds
Total
number
of
seeds
taken
×
100
For the current experiment, we have taken 20 sprouted seeds. So, the germination ability is 100 %.

Eq. (2) shows the germination index which depends mainly on average shoot length of seeds.
(2)
Germination
Index
=
N a
×
L a
N c
×
L c
×
100
Where Na and Nc are total number of germinated seeds in case of HCDs and control sample, respectively. While, La and Lc are average shoot length of seeds in case of HCDs and control sample, respectively.

The vigor index 1 was calculated using Eq. (3).
(3)
Vigor
Index
1 =
Germination
%
×
Length
of
seedling
( cm )
The germination% was 100 as the sprouted seeds were used for the experiment and length of shoot was considered as length of seedling.

The "percentage inhibition growth" in seed germination refers to the reduction or restriction in the growth of seeds compared to a control group calculated by using Eq. (4).
(4)
Percentage
Inhibition
Growth
=
C c
×
C a
C c
where, Ca and Cc is average root length of seeds present in HCDs and control sample, respectively.

The photocatalytic efficacy of synthesized HCDs was assessed through degradation of an aqueous solution containing a known concentration of RB dye under UV light irradiation. The concentration of photocatalyst was optimized and degradation was carried out at different pH. In this photocatalytic procedure, 10 mg of HCDs was introduced into a 40 mL of 10 ppm dye solution, employing a jacketed setup. Prior to initiation of UV light exposure, suspension underwent continuous stirring for a minimum of 60 min in darkness. This preliminary step ensured establishment of an adsorption-desorption equilibrium between RB dye and catalyst system. Upon attainment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the suspension comprising catalyst and RB dye was subjected to UV irradiation using a 300-Watt UV lamp. To maintain reaction solution at a constant temperature of 27 ± 1 °C, a cooling jacket enveloped the reactor during the photocatalytic reaction. Throughout 120-minute observation period, samples were collected at different intervals. Variations in absorbance within aliquots of RB dye solution during course of reaction were quantified through UV–Vis spectrophotometry. RB dye shows a characteristic UV peak at 547 nm confirmed from literature [24]. Degradation efficiency was determined using Eq. (5) as shown below:
(5)
Degradation
efficiency
( % )
=
A o
−
A t
A o
×
100
where At and Ao are concentrations of RB dye at any time t and 0 min of photocatalytic reaction [25]. This systematic methodology allowed for precise evaluation of photocatalytic degradation performance of HCDs in presence of RB dye under UV light illumination.

Loading capacity of the HCDs is defined as the amount of dopamine hydrochloride (DP) encapsulated per unit weight of the HCDs. The formula for calculating the loading capacity is shown at Eq. (6) as follows:
(6)
Loading
Capacity
( % )
=
Entrapped
DP
HCDs
weight
×
100
Entrapped DP is determined by UV absorbance spectra discussed in Section 3.4 and HCDs weight was taken as 10 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and morphological characterization of synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence properties of HCDs were examined using fluorescence spectroscopy across a range of excitation wavelengths from 320 to 480 nm (Fig. 2 c). Notably, the highest emission intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength (λexc) of 340 nm, corresponding to an emission peak (λemi) at 428 nm. This wavelength-dependent emission behavior can be attributed to the greater number of particles excited at this specific wavelength. Such behavior is commonly observed in various fluorescent carbon nanomaterials and is influenced by the surface properties and size of the HCDs. Moreover, the HCDs solution exhibited robust green emission when exposed to UV light at 254 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 2a displays a peak at 270 nm, representing the π-π* transition of the CC bond (carbon core of the sp2 domain), with a tail extending into the visible range without significant structures [26,27]. This absorption spectrum was then utilized to construct a Tauc's plot for the calculation of the bandgap energy, following Tauc's equation:
(7)
(
α h v
)
1 / n
= A
(
h v −
E g
)

Here “n” is an index which tells us nature of electronic transition, α is an absorption coefficient, hυ is energy of photon, A is a proportionality constant (usually taken as 1 for amorphous material), E g is permissible energy gap i.e., band gap. For case of indirect allowed transition, value of n becomes 2. Fig. 2b represent plot of (αhν)1/
n vs hν for n = 2 respectively. In this plot, linear region was extrapolated to x-axis to get bandgap which is 2.56 eV for synthesized HCDs [28]. This value provides valuable insights into optical properties and potential applications of HCDs. Generally, a precise control over band gap is crucial in tailoring optical and electronic properties of CDs, enabling their utilization in specific optoelectronic/ photocatalytic applications.

FTIR stands as a valuable tool for unrevealing molecular structures and their environmental context, courtesy of its sensitivity to different chemical bonds and functional groups within a molecule. In the FTIR spectrum of HCDs (Fig. 3 a), a broad band stretching between 3300 and 3500 cm−1 emerges, typically attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and NH2 groups. A relatively weak absorption related to CC bonds manifests as a broad feature centred at 2105 cm−1. Moreover, spectral region ranging from 1660 to 1600 cm−1 houses a peak centred at 1635 cm−1. This particular peak can be ascribed to vibrations of CC bonds found in inner core or CO groups present on surface of HCDs [22]. To further study chemical composition and chemical environment of HCDs, XPS measurements were taken. The HCDs consist mainly 57.05% carbon and 29.05% oxygen, 2.50% nitrogen, and only 1.05% sulfur. Fig. 3 presented the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, S 2p and N 1 s bands. C1s band is deconvoluted to peaks at 284.7, 288.38, 292.88 and 295.98 eV (Fig. 3c.), which are attributed to C–C/CC carbon network, CO/NO bonds, anhydride and O–CO respectively [29,30]. O 1 s band has only one peak at 531.6 which correspond to C–O–C/O–CO (Fig. 3f) [31]. N 1s peak at 399.78 eV (Fig. 3d.) is attributed to amide (N–CO) bond [31], NH (401.73), pyridinic N (398.37) and CN–C (397.28) [32].S 2p spectrum can be resolved into two distinct peaks at 168.6 and 169.4 eV, implies existence of C–SOx (x = 3, 4) on surface of synthesized CDs (Fig. 3e) [9,10,29]. These revelations harmoniously align with the insights gleaned from the FTIR analysis, collectively portraying as HCDs are predominantly comprising CC network with carboxylic acid derivatives (O–CO/N–CO) and have –OH/–NH/−SO x groups at surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and structure of HCDs
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM images showed that HCDs were well dispersed with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 4 a). Average size of synthesized CDs was 9.8 ± 1.54 nm. Hollow structure of synthesized CDs as revealed by HRTEM, adds an intriguing aspect to their functionality. Textural analysis was carried out for HCDs to know about nature of porosity in prepared materials. N2 adsorption measurements showed ink-bottle Type-IV adsorption isotherm which implies hollow structure of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 4e) [33] and are found to be consistent with HRTEM images. BJH model analysis indicated HCDs are hollow with specific surface area of 12.1 m2/g, pore volume of 3.81 × 10−3 cm3/g and stimulated pore diameter of 2.1 nm (see Fig. 4f).

The amorphous nature of HCDs is revealed by XRD graph (Fig. 5 a). The broad peak between 24.9° and 31.4° (2θ) implies (002) crystalline plane for hexagonal graphite [30]. The broad peak (1050‒3378 cm−1) in Raman spectrum suggests the presence of structural disorder, defects, or heterogeneity in the carbon lattice (Fig. 5b). This broad peak is deconvoluted to different bands G, D’, D’ + LA, 2D” and D + D’, where each associated with specific vibrational modes and structural elements. The G band represents the first-order Raman scattering associated with the vibration of carbon atoms in a sp²-hybridized lattice and it is a fundamental feature in the Raman spectra of carbon materials, including graphene and carbon dots. All other bands are of higher-order Raman modes related to the stacking order of graphene layers and their overtones [34].

In order to validate the unique hollow nature of the synthesized CDs, an encapsulation experiment was conducted, employing DP as a model compound. DP was selected as the encapsulant due to its distinct optical properties and well-established UV–Vis spectral characteristics. An aqueous solution of DP was prepared, and its absorption spectrum was recorded as a reference. Subsequently, the HCDs were introduced into the DP solution, and the mixture was allowed to incubate under controlled conditions. The solution was kept at room temperature for 24 hr under mild stirring to ensure complete encapsulation. The resulting solution was centrifuged to remove the free DP. Finally, UV was recorded to calculate the loading capacity. The absorption spectra of DP in distilled water and HCDs are presented in Fig. 6 . DP have shown a well-defined absorption peak at 279 nm as reported in literature [35]. The slight shift in the intensity and absorbance has been observed proving successful encapsulation of DP within the HCDs. To quantify the loading capacity of the HCDs for DP, the difference in absorbance between the reference DP solution and the solution post-incubation was determined. The loading capacity of HCDs was calculated and it was found to be 2.13 ± 0.12%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HCDs fluorescent probe for Fe3+ determination
ce:section.ce:para: The decrease in intensity of fluorescence was investigated for 12 types of metal ions. Among these, minor reductions in fluorescence were observed for ions like Ag+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, K+, Zn2+, Ag+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+and Hg2+, while Fe3+ exhibited most substantial fluorescence quenching (Fig. 7 a). This heightened quenching effect underscores HCDs’ preferential selectivity for Fe3+ over other metal ions.

Different quantities of Fe3+ ions in 0–50 µM range were introduced to a solution of CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) to test their sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions. With rise in Fe3+ concentration, fluorescence intensity decreases steadily, as can be noticed in Fig. 7b. Relative fluorescence response (F0/F) of HCDs as a function of Fe3+ concentration is also represented in Fig. 7d. Stern–Volmer plot depicts an ideal linear nature in 0–50 µM range of concentration, which further describes fluorescence quenching efficiency and calculated using Eq. (8) as follows:
(8)
F 0
/ F =
0.01847
[
C m
]
+
1.03339
where C m is Fe3+ concentration and F 0 and F represent HCDs fluorescence intensities without presence and in existence of Fe3+, respectively. Similarly, using equation 3σ/m, detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 1.1 µM, where m is slope of linear fit and σ is standard deviation of intercept [36]. LOD for Fe3+ detection by HCDs is comparable to previously published report [37]. Remarkably, this value stands below World Health Organization's permissible threshold for Fe3+ in drinking water (5.36 µM) [38]. Nagaraj et al. (2022) fabricated CDs from Borassus flabellifer (ice apple) using hydrothermal method and used as nanoprobe for the detection of Fe3+ with a detection limit of 2.01 μM [39]. The plausible mechanism in current study is fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism (Fig. 7c). FRET mechanism encompasses transmission of non-radiative energy from CDs to an energy acceptor, typically a quencher, positioned within a proximity of 10 to 100 nm. Hydroxyl groups present on surface of HCDs interact with Fe3+ ions to form a chelate complex. Also, absorption spectrum of Fe3+ overlaps with emission spectrum of HCDs. In this arrangement of HCDs and Fe3+, HCDs act as donor fluorophore, emitting fluorescence upon excitation. Fe3+ ions present in solution serve as acceptors of this fluorescence energy. Interactions between HCD and Fe3+ions facilitate energy transfer from excited state of HCDs to half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions, leading to discernible changes in HCDs’ fluorescence behavior. These changes could manifest as fluorescence quenching or shifts in emission spectra. Importantly, extent of these fluorescence alterations correlates with concentration of Fe3+ ions in solution. This inherent interaction-driven FRET process eliminates need for an external acceptor fluorophore, making it a direct and efficient approach for Fe3+ sensing using HCDs [2,3,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic studies for RB dye using HCDs
ce:section.ce:para: HCDs were used as a photocatalyst without the need for any external doping or semiconductor nanomaterials. The photocatalytic performance of HCDs under UV light was evaluated using photoactivity of RB in aqueous solution. Utilizing changes in UV–vis absorbance with response time, degradation of RB was investigated. Without HCDs, the characteristic absorbance of RB, which is centered at 547 nm, decreased by 31 % (Fig. S4) when exposed to UV. There are reports of other nanosystems having the same range of bandgap as the synthesized CDs which also displayed the degradation of RB [15,41]. The concentration of dye was same for all the experiments which was 40 mL of 10 ppm RB solution. The dosage of the photocatalyst i.e. HCDs was optimized. For that a series of experiments were conducted by varying the amount of HCDs added to 40 mL of 10 ppm RB dye solution. Different dosages, namely 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg of HCDs, were introduced into 40 mL of 10 ppm RB dye solution. The results demonstrated a dose-dependent effect on the degradation efficiency. Specifically, the addition of 5 mg of HCDs led to an 83 % degradation efficiency, which increased to 91 % with 10 mg of HCDs (Fig. 8 a). The observed initial rise in the rate of dye degradation with an increase in the catalyst amount can be attributed to the higher number of active sites available on the photocatalyst's surface. However, higher dosages showed a decrease in efficiency, with 82% for 15 mg and 72% for 20 mg of catalyst. This trend indicates that an excess amount of photocatalyst may hinder the degradation by potentially impeding the absorption of light crucial for the catalytic reaction. Based on these experiments, the optimized catalytic dosage for achieving the highest degradation efficiency was determined to be 10 mg of HCD. Further, the effect of pH was studied with the 10 mg of HCDs. It is evident from Fig. 8b that degradation is maximum at pH = 5.00. At lower pH values (e.g., pH = 3.00), the positively charged surfaces of both the catalyst and dye molecules lead to repulsion, reducing the extent of catalytic reaction. However, beyond pH = 5.00, the rate of dye degradation decreases. This is attributed to the anionic nature of RB, where at higher pH levels, repulsive forces between the catalyst surface and dye molecules come into play, resulting in a reduced rate of dye degradation [42]. The degradation graphs at different pH and their kinetics are shown in Fig. S2 and S3. HCDs showed a progressive decline in RB concentration over time, with a destructive efficiency of 91% within 120 min at pH = 5.00 (Fig. 8c) and obeys pseudo first order kinetics shown in Fig. 8d.

Radical trapping experiments were conducted to elucidate the involvement of active species, including holes (h+ ), superoxide anions (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in the photocatalytic degradation of RB dye. 2 mL of 10 mM each of tert. butyl alcohol (TBA), di-sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Na2EDTA) and para benzoquinone (PBQ) were employed as the radical scavengers for •OH, h+ , •O2 −, respectively. When PBQ is added into the RB solution, the degradation% reduced from 91% to 67%. The inhibition of RB photodegradation by TBA was more pronounced in comparison to the impact observed with the addition of Na2EDTA. This suggests that hydroxide radicals played a secondary role in the degradation process. Based on the experimental findings, it can be inferred that the predominant influence of active species in the degradation process follows the order •O2 −> •OH > h+ as illustrated in Fig. 9 a. The experimental results coupled with insights from existing literature, allow us to propose a plausible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RB dye [43]. HCDs, containing sp2 CC double bonds in their inner core, possess a remarkable ability to absorb UV and visible light through π–π* transitions. When exposed to light, HCDs absorb photons, inducing π–π* transition and generating electron-hole pairs. These excited electrons (e −) and holes (h+ ) are potent redox agents and play a pivotal role in a cascade of redox reactions. HCDs facilitate oxidation of dissolved oxygen and hydroxide ions present in dye solution into highly reactive radicals such as •O2 − and hydroxyl radicals •OH. These radicals, along with photogenerated electrons, actively engage with RB dye molecules, cleaving their chemical bonds and initiating degradation. This process can lead to the complete mineralization of RB dye into harmless end products like carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (H2O) (see Fig. 9c).

The reusability of the synthesized HCDs as a photocatalyst for the degradation of RB dye was systematically investigated over five consecutive cycles. The degradation efficiency observed for each cycle demonstrated the catalyst's robustness and stability under repeated use. Initially, the HCDs achieved a degradation efficiency of 91 % in the first cycle. HCDs were recovered from degraded dye solution by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm Subsequent cycles showed a gradual decrease in efficiency, with values of 87 %, 84 %, 79 %, and 75 % for the second, third, fourth, and fifth cycles, respectively (Fig. 9b). This decline in degradation efficiency can be attributed to partial loss of active sites over repeated cycles and mass loss of catalyst during recovery and reuse processes. The results underscore durability of HCDs and their viability as reusable photocatalysts in the degradation of organic pollutants.

EEO (Electrical Energy per Order) is the amount of electrical energy in kilowatt-hours (kWh) needed to decrease the concentration of dye by 90 % (one order of magnitude) in one cubic meter of contaminated water and is calculated using Eq. (9) as follows [44]:
(9)
E
E O
(
k W h ·
m
− 3
)
=
P ×
t
90 %
×
1000
V ×
60
× l o g
A o
A
t
90 %
t
90 %
=
ln
10
k
( for
first
order
reaction )

P is power of UV lamp which is 0.3 kWatt, t 90 % is extent of time needed for 90 % RB decontamination, k is pseudo first order rate constant of dye degradation reaction, V is volume of aqueous solution of dye in L (0.040 L), Ao is initial absorbance at 0 min and
A
t
90 %
is absorbance at t 90 % . The price for local electricity is 6.50 (INR) per unit. The related electricity cost (EC ) for decontamination of RB dye has been determined using Eq. (10). EEO and EC values are shown in Table 2
.
(10)
E C
=
P ×
t
90 %
×
6.50
60

The most sensitive stage of plant life cycle is the seed germination and nanoparticles affect the seed germination activity [47]. All seeds do not sprout at a same way. When chickpeas are sprouted, root emerges first and then it grows into shoot but sprouted wheat contains both shoot and root. The results of current study were quite intriguing. When different concentrations of HCDs solution were introduced into water alongside sprouted seeds, a remarkable effect on plant growth was observed. No shoots were grown in case of control group where sprouted wheat seeds were exposed to water alone. But long germinated roots, shoots and varying numbers of seminal roots were observed in HCD's solution. In fact, leaves were obtained in most of the seeds. Only a few shoots were grown in control sample of chickpeas. But in HCD's solution, there was a significant growth of shoot, root and leaves on 6th day of experiment (Fig. S5). Various parameters depending on shoot and root length are tabulated in Table S1. Variation of average root length, shoot length and germination index of wheat and chickpeas is shown in Fig. 10 . All the parameters were enhanced in the presence of HCDs. The variation of parameters within the different concentration of HCDs might be due to competition between aquaporins (channels responsible for water transport in seeds) and HCDs.

The plausible reasons for increase in germination index in presence of HCDs are depicted in Fig. 11 b. HCDs that are absorbed on surface of plant roots can potentially enter the plant's internal structure through pores present in cell wall and plasmodesmata. This movement can occur via both apoplastic and symplastic pathways, allowing HCDs to traverse cortex region. Endodermis, which consists of a single layer of cells, plays a crucial role in regulating the movement of water and ions. Passage cells within endodermis enable passage of water molecules from cortex to protoxylem, eventually reaching xylem vessels. Once reaching inside xylem, HCDs can be transported to various parts of plant. Once inside plant cells, they might interact with cellular components, influencing various processes, including photosynthesis. HCDs' ability to act as both electron acceptors and donors could potentially enhance electron transport rates within photosystems during photosynthesis. This might optimize flow of electrons through photosynthetic electron transport chain, leading to increased ATP and NADPH production. Also, HCDs could influence absorption and utilization of light energy. This might result in higher efficiency of light harvesting and energy conversion [21,48]. HCDs have the ability to serve as nanocarriers, transporting substances like growth-promoting chemicals or vital nutrients. The HCD surface is rich in hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) that bind water and essential plant nutrients like K +, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, and Zn2+ [31]. Thus, HCDs can improve seeds' ability to retain water and absorb nutrients. This study provides insights into the non-toxic and biocompatible nature of HCDs and can promote plant growth and vitality.

In this study, we successfully synthesized HCDs with a stimulated pore diameter of 2.1 nm using a straightforward pyrolysis method with CA as the precursor material. The versatility of HCDs was explored through various applications, highlighting their potential in environmental remediation and plant growth enhancement. Notably, our findings revealed that HCDs effectively degraded 91 % of RB dye within a short 120-minute timeframe under UV irradiation at pH = 5.00, showcasing their promising role in environmental remediation. Furthermore, HCDs exhibited remarkable selectivity and sensitivity in detecting Fe3+ ions, with a low detection limit of approximately 1.1 µM. Most intriguingly, when introduced into the growth medium, HCDs positively influenced the growth of chickpea and wheat sprouts, suggesting their potential as enhancers of overall plant health and productivity. These multifaceted applications establish HCDs derived from CA as valuable candidates for addressing environmental challenges, sensitive metal ion detection, and promoting sustainable agriculture.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Introducing various functional groups on the surface of HCDs can enhance their interaction with different pollutants (especially drugs), improving their efficiency in environmental remediation. Further research into their effectiveness and safety in these areas would be highly beneficial.

Different surface passivating agents can be added during synthesis to fabricate desired doped CDs. Doping of heteroatoms can increase optical properties of CDs, enhancing their sensitivity for detecting heavy metals.

Combining HCDs with other nanomaterials can create hybrid nanocomposites with enhanced properties. Such composites can offer improved stability, reusability, and overall performance in photocatalytic degradation and other applications.
The findings of seed germination provide valuable insights into the applicability of HCDs in agricultural practices and seed germination research.

The successful encapsulation of DP within the HCDs showcased their potential as versatile carriers for various bioactive compounds and drug delivery systems in future. Further research into their effectiveness and safety in these areas would be highly beneficial.

ce:section.ce:para: Arpita: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Parmod Kumar: Visualization, Validation, Resources. Ravi Kumar: Validation, Supervision. Chien-Te Hsieh: Visualization, Validation, Conceptualization. Kuan Shiong Khoo: Investigation, Formal analysis. Sandeep Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Arpita is thankful to University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India, for financial assistance through Junior Research Fellowship (JRF) (UGC Ref No: 114/ (CSIR-UGC NET JUNE 2017). Authors are also grateful to Department of Science & Technology, Delhi for providing financial assistance under DST PURSE Grant- SR/PURSE/2022/126.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104935.
